purpose: Draft (inactive) question objects derived from the Vinci 2026-07-07 verification map's 'quiz-question' and 'visual-question' facts. These are NOT part of src/content/course.json's live "questions" array and will not appear in the app until explicitly promoted there, after each question's verificationStatus is upgraded from draft-unverified and each fact is checked against Vinci's confirmed manual copy (per the verification map's needsVerification flag, which is true on every source fact).
activationRule: Do not add any of these questions to course.json unless: (1) its verificationStatus has been changed away from 'draft-unverified' following direct confirmation against the manual, and (2) for image-based questions, the image/imageAlt have been reviewed for exam relevance and remain within the copyright/publishing constraints noted in public/content/vinci/2026-07-07/README.md.
duplicateCheckNotes: Cross-referenced against all 133 live questions in src/content/course.json (not just chapter-2-using-lifeboats) before drafting. Deliberately excluded from this pack: lifeboat numbering odd/starboard-even/port (duplicates q-ch2-numbering-official-vh), propeller-handedness backing-to-port (duplicates q-ch2-backingport-hard), drain-plug-closed-before-launch (duplicates q-ch2-boatplug / q-ch2-drainplugfirst-hard), 24-hour motor-lifeboat fuel duration (duplicates q-ch2-24hrfuel), general painter-attachment location (duplicates q-ch2-seapainteruse), and bare pintle/gudgeon/stern-sheets vocabulary definitions (duplicate q-gloss-pintle, q-gloss-gudgeon, q-gloss-sternsheets). Where this pack still touches related hardware (toggle pin, bow-vs-stern-sheets coxswain position), the question is framed around a distinct fact the live bank does not already test.

questions.prompt: In the IMO/LSA safety-symbol pictogram system shown here, what's the difference between a GREEN pictogram and a BLUE/PURPLE pictogram?
questions.imageAlt: Photo of an IMO/LSA lifesaving safety symbol pictogram chart from the Vinci 2026-07-07 source batch

choices.text: Green marks safe-condition information -- life-saving equipment and its location is one such category, alongside others like escape-route direction signs; blue/purple instead marks an operating instruction
Green marks operating instructions; blue/purple marks equipment locations
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual color coding shown on the chart.

choices.text: Green is for personal flotation devices only; blue/purple is for signaling equipment only
choices.misconception: Invents a device-category split instead of the real function-based (safe-condition info vs. instruction) split.

choices.text: The color has no fixed meaning and varies by ship
choices.misconception: Assumes the pictograms aren't standardized, when IMO/LSA symbol color-coding is consistent across ships.

hints: Think about what the pictogram is telling you to do versus what it's simply labeling.
One color family marks 'where equipment is/what's safe'; the other marks 'what action to take.'
questions.explanation: On IMO/LSA lifesaving symbol charts, green pictograms mark safe-condition information. Life-saving equipment and its stowage location (lifeboat, liferaft, embarkation ladder, etc.) is one common category shown here -- the same green family also covers other safe-condition signs, such as escape-route direction markers (tested separately in this course, see q-ch1-imo-symbols-scenario-medium). Blue/purple pictograms, by contrast, mark operating instructions (fasten seat belts, release gripes, start engine, etc.) rather than equipment or safe-condition locations.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-07, image 2026-07-07_030301.jpg (chapter-1-planning-ahead, IMO/LSA safety symbol pictograms); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-07-verification-map.json. Revised 2026-07-09 per vinci-held-question-remediation-report.md to explicitly reconcile with the live q-ch1-imo-symbols-scenario-medium's escape-route framing, so the two green-pictogram questions read as complementary (different safe-condition sub-categories) rather than contradictory.

questions.prompt: What are 'limber holes' in a lifeboat's construction?

choices.text: Openings in the floors (the athwartship ribs in the bilge) that let bilge water flow between compartments without going through the outer shell
The buoyancy units mounted under the side benches
choices.misconception: Confuses limber holes (drainage) with buoyancy units (flotation).

choices.text: Holes drilled in the gunwale for rigging oars
choices.misconception: Invents an oar-related function limber holes don't serve.

choices.text: The drain-plug fitting at the bottom of the hull
choices.misconception: Confuses limber holes with the separate automatic drain / drain-plug fitting.

hints: Think about how water that's already inside the boat needs to move around, not how it gets out of the boat entirely.
They're cut into the metal ribs running side to side in the bottom of the boat.
questions.explanation: Limber holes are openings cut in a lifeboat's floors (the athwartship metal ribs in the bilge) that let bilge water flow freely between compartments without having to pass through the boat's outer shell plating.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-07, image 2026-07-07_041663-10.jpg (chapter-2-using-lifeboats, Buoyancy and Stability Features; Automatic Lifeboat Drain); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-07-verification-map.json

questions.prompt: A lifeboat's steering gear has two separate pin connections that are easy to mix up: a toggle pin, and a pintle-and-gudgeon pair. What's the key difference in what each one connects?

choices.text: The toggle pin holds the tiller onto the top of the rudder; the pintles and gudgeons are a completely separate pin-and-socket pair that hinge the rudder itself onto the boat's stern post
They're the same connection point -- just two different names for the same pin
choices.misconception: Conflates two distinct, separately located fittings as if they were one.

choices.text: The pintles and gudgeons hold the tiller to the rudder; the toggle pin hinges the rudder to the stern post
choices.misconception: Reverses which fitting connects to which part.

choices.text: Both the toggle pin and the pintle/gudgeon pair hinge the rudder to the stern post, just at different heights
choices.misconception: Misses that the toggle pin's job is the tiller connection entirely, not a second stern-post hinge point.

hints: There are two separate connection points on a lifeboat's steering gear: one for the tiller, and one for the rudder itself.
The rudder-to-boat connection uses a pin-and-socket pair, not the same pin that holds the tiller.
questions.explanation: These are two separate fittings that are easy to mix up: a toggle pin holds the tiller onto the top of the rudder, while pintles and gudgeons (a pin-and-socket pair) hold the rudder itself onto the boat's stern post, forming the rudder's hinge.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-07, image 2026-07-07_041663-12.jpg (chapter-2-using-lifeboats, Steering Equipment -- tiller, rudder, pintle, gudgeon, Kort Nozzle, steering wheel); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-07-verification-map.json. Revised 2026-07-09 per vinci-held-question-remediation-report.md: originally framed so the correct answer restated the already-live pintle/gudgeon fact (q-gloss-pintle, q-gloss-gudgeon) instead of testing the toggle pin. Reframing to test the toggle pin directly was considered, but q-quiz1a-toggle-pin-tiller (promoted 2026-07-09) now already tests exactly that fact, so this question was rewritten as a compare-and-contrast between the two fittings instead -- the only angle neither existing live question covers.

questions.prompt: A lifeboat's propeller can create an unwanted sideways 'paddlewheel effect.' When does this effect occur?

choices.text: When the propeller is running in reverse (backing down), not while propelling forward
Only while propelling forward at full throttle
choices.misconception: Reverses when the effect actually occurs.

choices.text: Only when the engine is first started, regardless of gear
choices.misconception: Unrelated to gear direction, which is what actually triggers the effect.

choices.text: Equally in both forward and reverse gear
choices.misconception: Ignores that the effect is specifically a reversing-gear phenomenon.

hints: Propellers are shaped to work efficiently in one direction of travel.
This effect is exactly why backing a lifeboat under power requires extra rudder correction.
questions.explanation: Propellers are designed to work efficiently producing thrust while propelling forward. When reversed, they operate inefficiently and generate an unwanted side thrust known as the 'paddlewheel effect' -- which is why backing a lifeboat under power requires compensating rudder action.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-07, image 2026-07-07_041663-14.jpg (chapter-2-using-lifeboats, Engine starting/stopping procedures; propeller-characteristics intro); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-07-verification-map.json
